_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 1
questions.prompt: Which stream operation keeps only items that pass a condition?
questions.explanation: Filter receives a condition and keeps values for which that condition is true. Map changes values instead of deciding whether to keep them.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 2
questions.explanation: Map replaces each String with the Integer returned by `length()`. Ada has three characters and Grace has five.

questions.title: Trace Lambdas & Streams
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The filter keeps passing scores. The map adds five points to each remaining score. `toList` starts the work and gathers the results. The resulting output is '[77, 95]'.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams practice 5
questions.prompt: What is wrong with `scores.stream().filter(score -> score >= 60);` on its own?
choices: Without a terminal operation like toList, the filter never actually runs
filter is not a real stream method
It modifies the original scores list in place
It always returns an empty list
questions.explanation: Stream steps like `filter` and `map` are lazy: they describe work but do nothing until a terminal operation such as `toList`, `count`, or `forEach` pulls the values through. The version with `.toList()` produces the result; the bare filter just builds and discards a pipeline. Streams never change the source list.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams concept check 1
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "A loop may be clearer"?
choices: Use a regular loop when the work needs several changing variables, early exits, detailed error handling, or many side effects. Streams fit best when data moves through a short set of clear steps.
questions.explanation: A lambda is a short function value, such as `name -> name.length()`. A stream processes collection data through steps like `filter`, `map`, and `sorted`, then finishes with an operation such as `toList`, `count`, or `forEach`. That reasoning supports the answer: Use a regular loop when the work needs several changing variables, early exits, detailed error handling, or many side effects. Streams fit best when data moves through a short set of clear steps.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams concept check 2
questions.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable Lambdas & Streams practice?
choices: Use a lambda for one small action.
questions.explanation: A lambda is a short function value, such as `name -> name.length()`. A stream processes collection data through steps like `filter`, `map`, and `sorted`, then finishes with an operation such as `toList`, `count`, or `forEach`. That reasoning supports the answer: Use a lambda for one small action.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams concept check 3
questions.prompt: Which recommendation belongs in a review of Lambdas & Streams code?
choices: Read a stream from top to bottom as data steps.
questions.explanation: A lambda is a short function value, such as `name -> name.length()`. A stream processes collection data through steps like `filter`, `map`, and `sorted`, then finishes with an operation such as `toList`, `count`, or `forEach`. That reasoning supports the answer: Read a stream from top to bottom as data steps.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams concept check 4
questions.prompt: Which guideline helps prevent a common Lambdas & Streams mistake?
choices: Use filter to keep and map to change.
questions.explanation: A lambda is a short function value, such as `name -> name.length()`. A stream processes collection data through steps like `filter`, `map`, and `sorted`, then finishes with an operation such as `toList`, `count`, or `forEach`. That reasoning supports the answer: Use filter to keep and map to change.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams concept check 5
questions.prompt: A classmate is checking their understanding of Lambdas & Streams. Which advice is correct?
questions.explanation: A lambda is a short function value, such as `name -> name.length()`. A stream processes collection data through steps like `filter`, `map`, and `sorted`, then finishes with an operation such as `toList`, `count`, or `forEach`. That reasoning supports the answer: Finish with a terminal operation.

questions.title: Lambdas & Streams concept check 6
questions.prompt: Which choice shows sound reasoning about Lambdas & Streams?
choices: Choose a loop when the stream would hide the real work.
questions.explanation: A lambda is a short function value, such as `name -> name.length()`. A stream processes collection data through steps like `filter`, `map`, and `sorted`, then finishes with an operation such as `toList`, `count`, or `forEach`. That reasoning supports the answer: Choose a loop when the stream would hide the real work.
